name: Epic FHIR API
what_it_does: Epic's FHIR R4 API for accessing and writing patient health data from Epic EHR systems, including demographics, medications, lab results, and clinical notes.
use_cases: Reading patient demographics, allergies, conditions, and medications for clinical decision support agents
Accessing structured lab results and vital signs via Observation resources
Writing clinical notes back to Epic using DocumentReference or NoteWriter APIs
Scheduling automation via Appointment and Slot resources
Population health analytics by querying bulk FHIR export for cohort data
not_for: Non-healthcare applications — requires patient data agreements and HIPAA compliance
Real-time streaming of clinical events (use Epic Interconnect or HL7 feeds)
Access to non-Epic EHR systems (each EHR has its own FHIR server)
best_when: Building clinical decision support tools, care coordination agents, or patient-facing apps for Epic-based health systems.
avoid_when: You don't have a signed BAA with the health system and explicit data sharing agreements; HIPAA violation risk is severe.

auth.notes: SMART on FHIR OAuth2: patient-context (patient standalone), EHR launch (embedded in Epic), backend services (client_credentials with JWK). Scopes follow SMART v1 (patient/*.read) or v2 (system/Patient.read) patterns.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Epic Sandbox (open.epic.com) is free for development with synthetic patient data
pricing.paid_tiers: Production access requires contract with Epic customer health system — not directly priced by Epic
pricing.notes: Costs negotiated with individual health system customers. Epic charges health systems for API access tiers. Third-party app certification may require Epic App Orchard review.

performance.rate_limits: Varies by health system and contract; typically 100-1000 requests/minute

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: FHIR OperationOutcome returned for errors with issue.code and issue.details; often verbose XML or JSON
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: FHIR PUT and conditional creates support idempotent operations via If-None-Exist header
known_agent_gotchas: Each health system has its own Epic instance with different base URL — no single global endpoint
SMART on FHIR token scopes must match what was requested at app registration — mismatch causes opaque 403 errors
Bulk FHIR export ($export) is async — poll status endpoint, then download NDJSON files; takes minutes to hours
Epic adds proprietary extensions to standard FHIR resources — parse with extension awareness or data is incomplete
Sandbox synthetic data (open.epic.com) behaves differently from production — test with real system in UAT environment

security_score_components.security_notes: SMART on FHIR is a rigorous auth standard. Patient data requires BAA and PHI handling agreements. JWK-based client assertion for backend services is very secure.
